verdict_reasoning: HMGB1 is canonically a nuclear DNA-binding protein (HMG-box architectural factor). However, it is actively secreted by activated immune cells and passively released from necrotic/pyroptotic cells, after which it acts as a prototypical DAMP/alarmin. Extracellular HMGB1 engages surface receptors (RAGE, TLR2/4, CXCR4) and is the target of therapeutic neutralizing antibodies — but the relevant pool is soluble extracellular ligand, not a wash-resistant surface-anchored form. Cell-state-induced release during stress/cell death/immune activation, with no intrinsic membrane anchor, fits cell_state_induced contextual display of the protein in the extracellular milieu (though access to the protein body via the cell surface is limited to its transient receptor-bound state).
predicted_key_uncertainty: Borderline with secreted_only: extracellular HMGB1 is largely soluble DAMP; surface presence is via transient receptor binding rather than stable anchoring.
